coredata.dc:title: IRMOF-3: A fluorescent nanoscale metal organic frameworks for selective sensing of glucose and Fe (III) ions without any modification
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Amit
Chowdhuri, Angshuman Ray
Kumari, Archana
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The amine functionalized isoreticular metal-organic framework-3 (IRMOF-3) is synthesized by hydrothermal method. Till now, it's widely used in the area of gas separation, adsorption, and catalysis due to large surface area, structural stability, and tunability. Here, we have reported the use of fluorescent nanoscale IRMOF-3 for highly selective detection of glucose as well as Fe3+ ions without any modification. This is due to NH2 and COOH groups are present on the surface of IRMOF-3 to bind cis-diols of the glucose molecule via host-guest interaction, and Fe3+ ions via ligand to metal charge transfer. The Synthesized IRMOF-3 has average diameter of 160 ± 20 nm and interestingly possess deep blue fluorescent emission spectra at 460 nm with quantum yield 17.3%. Using fluorometric assay, the limit of detection (LOD) of glucose and Fe3+ ions was found to be 0.56 μM and 4.2 nM respectively. More importantly, the synthesized IRMOF-3 is also utilized for detection of glucose and Fe3+ ions in bio-environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose sensing
IRMOF-3
Fe3+ ions sensing
Real samples analysis
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoluminescence and UV–Vis spectra of IRMOF-3
Fluorometric detection of glucose by IRMOF-3
Fluorometric detection of Fe3+ ions by IRMOF-3
Detection of glucose in human blood serum
Detection of Fe3+ ions in human blood serum
Detection of Fe3+ ions in real water samples
Surface morphology and particle size analysis
Possible mechanism for sensing of glucose and Fe3+ ions by the IRMOF-3

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Rajasthan
Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the DST, Government of India ( SB/FT/CS-068/2013 ) and Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photoluminescence spectra of IRMOF-3 at different excitation wavelength, (b) fluorescence and [inset: UV visible] spectra of IRMOF-3 at same concentration, (c) UV visible spectra of IRMOF-3 in presence of different analytes, and (d) fluorescent spectra of IRMOF-3 in different analytes and [inset shows corresponding bar diagram].

(a) & (c) represents bar diagram of different analytes and different metal ions respectively and corresponding fluorescent spectra of the IRMOF-3 after addition with different concentration of (b) (1–500 μM) glucose and (d) (0.1–10 μM) of Fe3+ ions.
Fluorometric detection of (a) Glucose and (b) Fe3+ ions in human blood serum as well as Fe3+ ions in real water sample [(c) Mineral, (d) Tap and (e) Pond water] by IRMOF-3.
Visual detection of (a) glucoses, and (b) Fe3+ ions, the fluorescent colour are decreases after addition of the different concentration of glucose and Fe3+ ions under UV light (365 nm).
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of IRMOF-3.
(a) FESEM image, (b) EDX spectrum [inset elemental composition], (c) HRTEM image, and (d) DLS of IRMOF-3.
(a): Fluorescent lifetime decay and (b) Zeta potential value of the IRMOF-3 after addition of glucose and Fe3+ ions.
Schematic synthetic illustration of IRMOF-3 for glucose and Fe3+ ions detection.
Working principle of glucose sensing by IRMOF-3.
Results show detection of glucose in human blood serum samples.

head.ce:title: IRMOF-3: A fluorescent nanoscale metal organic frameworks for selective sensing of glucose and Fe (III) ions without any modification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, 826004, Jharkhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Indian Institute of Petroleum (CSIR-IIP), Dehradun 248005, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The amine functionalized isoreticular metal-organic framework-3 (IRMOF-3) is synthesized by hydrothermal method. Till now, it's widely used in the area of gas separation, adsorption, and catalysis due to large surface area, structural stability, and tunability. Here, we have reported the use of fluorescent nanoscale IRMOF-3 for highly selective detection of glucose as well as Fe3+ ions without any modification. This is due to NH2 and COOH groups are present on the surface of IRMOF-3 to bind cis-diols of the glucose molecule via host-guest interaction, and Fe3+ ions via ligand to metal charge transfer. The Synthesized IRMOF-3 has average diameter of 160 ± 20 nm and interestingly possess deep blue fluorescent emission spectra at 460 nm with quantum yield 17.3%. Using fluorometric assay, the limit of detection (LOD) of glucose and Fe3+ ions was found to be 0.56 μM and 4.2 nM respectively. More importantly, the synthesized IRMOF-3 is also utilized for detection of glucose and Fe3+ ions in bio-environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have generated intense interest over the past two decades, due to their attractive potential applications in adsorption [1], gas storage [2], catalysis [3], and sensing [4–6]. Being porous materials, recently MOFs have shown great potential in selective detection of organic solvents [7], poisonous gases [8], biomolecules [9], and heavy metals [10] because of the advantages of high sensitivity and short response time. The particle size of MOFs controlled in nanoscale range has opened a new way in the field of nanotechnology [11]. In recent years, augmented efforts can be seen for the synthesis of NMOFs for numerous potential applications by virtue of their tunable surface functionalities and high porosity [12, 13]. Among the hundreds of known NMOFs, the isoreticular metal-organic framework (IRMOF-3) particularly has attracted interest due to their enormous porosity and enhanced stability [12]. Recently, Zhao et al. synthesized core−shell Pd@IRMOF-3 nanostructures as multifunctional catalysts for cascade reactions [3]. Kim et al. have developed carbothermal reduction of Ti-modified IRMOF-3 as a promising catalyst for oxidative desulfurization of diesel [14]. Again, Saha et al. also developed palladium anchored IRMOF-3 for Suzuki and Stille coupling reactions under heterogeneous condition [15]. Similarly, we have also prepared functionalized magnetic IRMOF-3 for targeted drug delivery [16]. Several pioneering studies demonstrate the great potential of IRMOF-3 either as catalyst [17] or drug carrier [16]. Very recently Bhardwaj et al. synthesized IRMOF-3 conjugated bacteriophage as a novel opto-sensor for S. arlettae [14]. In our awareness, however, no attempt has been made to utilize IRMOF-3 as a sensing material, which has highly inherent fluorescent property. In this work, we are utilizing a direct strategy for selective sensing of biomolecule and metal ion using IRMOF-3 that can enhance the utility of NMOFs.

Glucose exists in human blood and its concentration level is one of the most representative characteristics for diagnosis. From last 20 years, development of rapid and accurate method for detection of glucose concentration has attracted significant attention due to its importance in biomedical analysis for effective treatment of many biological disorders. Up to now, many attempts have been made to develop both enzymatic [4] and non-enzymatic [18] based glucose sensors. Many conventional techniques, such as colorimetry [19], electrochemical [20], electrochemiluminescence [21] fluorescence [22-25], and chromatography [26] have been employed for glucose determination. Among them, fluorescence method has been widely used for glucose sensing due to its high sensitivity, selectivity and low detection of limit [24].

In the past two years, numerous types of versatile fluorescent materials have been developed for glucose detection [9, 22]. Among them, a number of fluorescent materials have been widely used with association of enzymes to fabricate sensors for detection of the products from reactions catalyzed by corresponding oxidase. For example, Barrio et al. developed a fluorometric biosensor for continuous monitoring of glucose levels in biological samples based on near-infrared (NIR) excitation [4]. Muhr et al. synthesized europium-doped GdVO4 nanocrystals as a luminescent probe for enzymatic sensing of glucose [23]. Hu et al. synthesized fluorescent hydrogen peroxide sensor based on cupric oxide nanoparticles for glucose detection [9]. Azizi et al. developed a novel fluorescence method using water-soluble CdSe quantum dots for the fluorometric determination of glucose [24]. However, in these reported methods not only multiple steps but also expensive enzymes were involved to detect glucose. Recently, some fluorescent materials have been reported, where glucose is detected directly by quenching mechanism without use of any enzyme. For instance, Cordes et al. developed fluorescent quantum dots with boronic acid to sense glucose in aqueous Solution [25]. Shen et al. successfully synthesized one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent sensing of glucose [22]. Krishna et al. developed carbon dot based non enzymatic approach for the detection and estimation of glucose in blood serum [18]. Wang et al. synthesized carbon dot-immobilized hybrid microgels for optical detection of glucose using near infra-red light [27]. However, all aforementioned systems utilized boronic acid for glucose detection. As we know, so far, there is no report on the fabrication of fluorescent material without involving boronic acid for glucose detection. In this work, we have synthesized IRMOF-3 in single step, which has inherent fluorescent property used for selective sensing of glucose as well as metal ion without any surface modification and post processing steps.

Taking advantage of the excellent fluorescent properties of IRMOF-3, here we have extended the applications as a sensor for selective metal ion sensing. Ferric ion (Fe3+) is an essential transition metal ion in human body and it plays crucial roles in biological systems [28]. Either deficiency or excess amount of Fe3+ ions may cause several disorders and severe diseases like Parkinson's, anaemia, inflammation and heart failure [28]. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ ion is of great importance in numerous therapeutic protocols. In the present work, we demonstrate the use of IRMOF-3 as a fluorescent sensor without any modification as shown in Scheme 1 . These IRMOFs is highly stable in water with quantum yield 17.3 leading to their extensive biological applications, as demonstrated by the sensitive and selective detection of glucose and Fe3+ ions.

Zinc nitrate dihydrate {Zn(NO3)2.2H2O, 99%}, N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), glucose (C6H12O6), fructose (C6H12O6), sucrose (C12H22O11), urea (N2H4CO), Sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium iodide (KI), potassium bromide (KBr), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), ammonium thiocyanate (NH4SCN) and ascorbic acid (C6H8O6) was purchased from Merk India. Absolute ethanol (C2H5OH) was obtained from Merk Germany. Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), 2-amino-1, 4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (NH2-BDC), glutathione (C10H17N3O6S), dopamine (4-(2-Aminoethyl) benzene-1, 2-diol), citric acid (C6H8O7) was purchased from TCI India. Ferric chloride (FeCl3), nickel nitrate (NiNO3)2·6H2O, magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2·6H2O), cobalt nitrate (CoNO3)2.6H2O, lead sulphate (PbSO4), copper sulphate (CuSO4)·5H2O, mercuric chloride (HgCl2), cadmium sulphate (CdSO4·H2O), Sodium fluoride (NaF), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), stannous chloride (SnCl2·2H2O) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich Chemicals, USA and also dialysis membrane (MWCO 14 KD) was acquired from Sigma Aldrich Chemicals, USA. Distilled water was utilized for the whole experiments.

IRMOF-3 was synthesized by the previously reported method with slight modification [29]. Approximately, 66.93 mg of zinc nitrate dihydrate {Zn (NO3)2·2H2O} and 16.29 mg 2-amino-1, 4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (NH2-BDC) was dissolved in 5 mL of N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF). Then, 0.20 g polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) was slowly added into the above solution and heated at 100 °C for 8 h in Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After that, it was cooled down to room temperature; the reaction mixture was centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 mins. The obtained reddish yellow product was washed with DMF several times and dialysed against distilled water (MWCO 14 k Da) for 12 h to remove the unreacted materials. The obtained IRMOF-3 was dried in vacuum oven (60 °C) overnight.

Detection of glucose was carried out at room temperature by the fluorometric method. 200 μL of IRMOF-3 was added into 800 μL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 7.4, and then 500 μL of different analytes' solution (glucose, fructose, sucrose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, urea, citric acid, and glutathione) was added into solution. After that, the mixture solution was sonicated with moderate intensity at room temperature for 10 min to speed up the interaction between analytes and IRMOF-3. Finally, the fluorescent spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength of 360 nm. It was observed that the fluorescent intensity was least in case of glucose, which confirmed the selectivity of glucose.

The sensing of Fe3+ ions was carried out at room temperature by fluorometric method. At first, 200 μL of IRMOF-3 was added to the 800 μL of PBS at pH 7.4, and 500 μL different metals ions solution (Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Na+, Hg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, and Fe3+) was added into solution. Then the mixture solution was sonicated with reasonable intensity at room temperature for 10 mins to speed up the aggregation reaction between metal ions and IRMOF-3. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the fluorescent intensity was lowest in case of Fe3+ ions.

To determine the Fe3+ ions detection in environmental water by the IRMOF-3, water samples were collected from our laboratory tap, local pond outside the IIT (ISM) campus, Dhanbad India, and mineral water was purchased from the local shop. Pond and laboratory water samples were filtered by 0.22 μM filter paper to remove any solid suspension and mineral water was used without any filtration. Water samples were spiked with different concentration of Fe3+ ions (10, 20, and 30 nM). The solutions were analysed according to the given method.

For determination of glucose and Fe3+ ions in human blood serum, the blood serum was collected from IIT (ISM) health centre Dhanbad (Jharkhand) India. The serum was firstly treated with trichloroacetic acid (TCA) to release Fe3+ ions from protein according to a reported procedure [30]. 5 mL of 25% TCA was added to 5 mL serum, and then the mixture was centrifuged for 10 mins to eliminate protein because of reduce nonspecific interaction of the IRMOF-3 with proteins. The resulting solution was spiked with different amounts of glucose (10, 20, and 30 μM) and Fe3+ ions (10, 20, and 30 nM). The solution was analysed, according to the above mentioned procedure.

The fluorescence emission spectra of IRMOF-3 were recorded by Perkin Elmer LS 55 spectrophotometer. The IR spectrum of the IRMOF-3 was measured by the Agilent carry 660 instrument fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and scan times of 64 cm/s. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded with D/MAX2200PC Rigaku powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with CuKa1 radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The microstructure of the IRMOF-3 were examined by Supra 55 with Air Lock chamber for scientific research field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM;) and High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA) was performed with an accelerating voltage at 200 kV. The UV–Visible spectra of IRMOF-3 were characterized by Perkin-Elmer 950, spectrophotometer. Size distributions and zeta potential analysis were carried out on a Nanosizer (ZEN3600, Malvern). The fluorescent life time decay was analysed by Fluoro Max-4 fluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence and UV–Vis spectra of IRMOF-3
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of synthesized IRMOF-3 are shown in Fig. 1(a). It is observed that the fluorescent emission peak of IRMOF-3 had possesses red-shift with increase in excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm. The maximum fluorescent intensity is detected at excitation wavelength 360 nm, after that emission peak decreased outstandingly. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at this particular wavelength due to excitation of large number of particles [16]. The fluorescence spectrum of IRMOF-3 show maximum emission peak at 460 nm and corresponding UV–Visible absorption spectrum at 355 nm (sharp absorption peak) are shown in Fig. 1(b) [16]. This observed absorption peak is due to n-π* electronic transition of COOH group present on the surface of IRMOF-3. As shown in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, the B (benzenoid) band of the n-π* transitions of IRMOF-3 appears at 355 nm, and incorporation of different analytes (glucose, fructose, sucrose, and dopamine) only addition of glucose causes a different degree of blue shift of the B-bands. Particularly in case of glucose which decreases the B-band is shifted to lower wavelength near 330 nm. This is because two cis-diols of glucose combined with NH2 and COOH groups of IRMOF-3 moieties easily as compared to other analytes (fructose, sucrose and dopamine) [31] as shown in Fig. 1(c). At the excitation wavelength 360 nm, glucose posses' lower absorbance value to exhibit stronger quenching effects on the luminescence intensity of the IRMOF-3. Among fluorescence spectra of different analytes, only glucose shows remarkably quenched in the fluorescence spectra of the IRMOF-3 as shown in Fig. 1(d). But when addition of Fe3+ ions, no shift of B-bands because no host-guest interaction between the Fe3+ ions and carboxylic & amino groups of the IRMOF-3 moieties, but here may be ligand to metal charge transfer mechanism was occurred as shown in Fig. S-4. The bluish fluorescence colour of IRMOF-3 is observed by the naked eye under UV light (365 nm). But when different analytes are added, the fluorescent colour is noticeably changed only in case of glucose as shown in Fig. S-2(a). We are also using different types of NMOFs {IRMOF-3 [32], UIO-66 (NH2) [33], MIL101 [34], and ZIF-8 [35]} for glucose sensing, but interestingly observed that only IRMOF-3 is applicable for selective detection of glucose as shown in Fig. S-3. The effect pH solution on the fluorescence spectra of IRMOF-3 is shown in Fig. S-7. The fluorescence intensity of IRMOF-3 decreases in acidic medium (pH 2.0–pH 4), almost constant at (pH 6.0–pH 8.0) and further increase in pH value fluorescence intensity was decreased, which shows that fluorescence spectra of IRMOF-3 was depend on pH solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of glucose by IRMOF-3
ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is major source of energy in cells metabolism; it has significant effect on cell growth, but excess of glucose causes certain disease in human body like diabetes and cancers. So, detection of glucose is considered as an active area of research. In this work, fluorometric method is used for selective glucose detection by adding IRMOF-3 into the different analytes (ascorbic acid, glucose, fructose, sucrose, dopamine, urea, citric acid, and glutathione) solutions. The histograms are plotted F/F0 against equal concentration (500 μM) of different analytes. It is observed that glucose shows significant changes in emission spectra of IRMOF-3 at excitation wavelength 360 nm. This histogram indicates that the synthesized IRMOF-3 has high affinity and selectivity towards glucose as shown in Fig. 2(a). Among the various analytes only glucose is best quencher of fluorescence intensity in presence of the IRMOF-3 in aqueous medium. We expected that the, amine and carboxylic groups of the IRMOF-3 may have formed stable complex with cis-diol of glucose via host-guest interaction [22, 31]. Each glucose molecule has two pairs of cis-diol units in the 1, 2 and 5, 6-positons which is key structural feature that confers a glucose response to IRMOF-3. These cis-diols covalently bridge to the –NH2 group present on the surface of the IRMOF-3 as shown in Scheme 2 . Compared to glucose, other analytes have no two pairs of cis-diol units to bind easily with IRMOF-3. The fluorescence intensity of the IRMOF-3 is well quenched, which is may be considered as surface induced quenching mechanism.

Increase in concentration of glucose shows gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with minimum at concentration of 500 μM. No noticeable change in fluorescence intensity of IRMOF-3 is observed at high concentration (500 μM) of other analytes. The fluorescence intensity of IRMOF-3 decreased linearly with the logarithmic concentrations of glucose over the linear range 1–225 μM as shown in Fig. 2(b) & Fig. S-5(a). The limit of detection was 0.56 μM, based on 3SD/k (signal-to-noise ratio 3). This was calculated from calibration curve for F0/F = 14 [glucose] + 106 with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.996 as shown in Fig. S-5(a) and comparable with reported methods as shown in (Table S-1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of Fe3+ ions by IRMOF-3
ce:section.ce:para: For selective detection of Fe3+ ions by fluorometric method was carried out by adding different metal salts (Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Na+, Hg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, and Fe3+) solution into the IRMOF-3. The histograms are plotted against equal concentration (10 μM) of different metal ions. We observed that only Fe3+ ions show the significant change in emission spectra of IRMOF-3 at excitation wavelength 360 nm as shown in histogram in Fig. 2(c). This histogram shows that the synthesized IRMOF-3 have high affinity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions. This is due to nitrogen atom of amine groups of the IRMOF-3 can donate electron pairs to the vacant d-orbital of the Fe3+ ions and make surface complexation by ligand to metal charge transfer [34]. The sensing effects of IRMOF-3 towards different anions are shown in Fig. S-6.

The fluorescence intensity of IRMOF-3 regularly decreased with increase in concentration of Fe3+ ions (0.1–10 μM) as shown in Fig. 2(d). Decrease in fluorescence intensity of IRMOF-3 shows a linear response with increasing Fe3+ ions concentration in the range of 0.1–4.0 μM as shown in Fig. S-5(b). The relative intensity (F/F0) against increasing Fe3+ ions concentration shows outstanding linearity with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.991, shown in Fig. S-5(b). The LOD was calculated from the calibration curve for F0/F = 22 [Fe3+ ions] + 104 and found to be 4.2 nM based on (3SD/k), which is smaller than the previously reported procedure, shown in Table S-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in human blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of unknown amount of glucose in human blood serum was determined by using standard addition technique with stock solution of glucose (500 μM). The sample containing blood serum (50 μL) and 2 mL of IRMOF-3 (1 mg/mL) sample was spiked with different concentrations (10, 20, and 30 μM) of glucose (1 mL). The samples were incubated at 25 °C for 35 min and then fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at excitation of 360 nm as shown in Fig. 3(a). Glucose concentrations obtained by the projected technique is well in accordance with value calculated by a commercial glucose meter [Dr. Morepen Glucose Monitor (BG-03)] shown in Table 1 . The IRMOF-3 shows high sensitivity towards glucose with LOD 0.56 μM, which is much lower than the typical concentration of glucose in human blood serum (4.4 to 6.1 mM/L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in human blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of unknown amount of Fe3+ ions in human blood serum was analysed by the standard addition technique with 10 μM stock solutions of Fe3+ ions. Different concentrations (10, 20, and 30 nM) of Fe3+ ions (1 mL) were directly added to a mixture of 2 mL IRMOF-3 (1 mg/mL) solution and 0.5 mL deproteinized blood serum in a 5 mL volumetric flask. Then the mixture was diluted to 5 mL with potassium phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4). The resulting solution was incubated at 25 °C for 35 min and then emission spectra was recorded at excitation of 360 nm as shown in Fig. 3(b). The IRMOF-3 shows high sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions with LOD 4.2 nM, which is much lower than the typical concentration of Fe3+ ions in human blood serum (5–39 μM) [28] as shown in Table-S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To establish practical usability of IRMOF-3 for Fe3+ ions sensing in real water samples by fluorometric method. At first, pond water was filtered to eliminate several solid suspensions, whereas mineral water and tap water were used without filtration. No Fe3+ ions were detected in real water samples (pond water, tap water, and mineral water) in fluorescence spectra. Then standard addition technique was used. Three different amounts (10, 20, and 30 nM) of Fe3+ ions were spiked to real water samples and then fluorescence spectra were recorded at 360 nm. The fluorescence intensity decreased after addition of Fe3+ ions into all water samples are shown in Fig. 3[(c), (d), and (e)].

In this study, the visual detection of glucose was carried out under UV light (365 nm). Glucose concentration was increases from 100 to 500 μM, the fluorescence colour of IRMOF-3 changed from light blue to dark blue with increase in concentration of glucose. This observation is visualised by naked eyes. This indicates that the prepared IRMOF-3 can be used for visual detection of glucose as shown in Fig. 4(a).

In this study, the visual detection of Fe3+ ions was carried out under UV light (365 nm). When Fe3+ ions concentration was increased from 2 to 10 μM, fluorescence colour of IRMOF-3 changed from light blue to dark blue. This is also observed by naked eyes. This shows that the prepared IRMOF-3 can be used for visual detection of Fe3+ ions as shown in Fig. 4(b).

The FTIR spectrum of synthesized IRMOF-3 is shown in Fig. 5(a). The characteristic peak around 3400 cm−1 is observed due to primary NH2 group present on the surface of IRMOF-3. Slight peak shifting from 3500 cm−1 to 3400 cm−1 is due to formation of intra-framework hydrogen bonding between primary amine and OH of the carboxylic group inside the IRMOF-3 [3]. The characteristic peak at 2950 cm−1 is for CH stretching of alkyl group, peaks at 900 cm−1 and 1280 cm−1 are due to the CH bending and CN stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 are due to CO stretching frequency of carboxylic group and NH bending of primary amine group respectively.

The phase purity, degree of crystallinity, and physical properties of the IRMOF-3 were characterized by the powder XRD as shown in Fig. 5(b). A well-known broad XRD diffraction pattern close to 2θ ≈ 22° shows the amorphous nature of IRMOF-3. This is due to numerous disorders of PVP linkers into the IRMOF-3. Spherical morphology of IRMOF-3 is found due to the different degree of disorders of the two organic linkers PVP and NH2-BDC. It is reported that, if the concentration of PVP is more, particles are amorphous in nature but when the concentration of NH2-BDC is enhanced, particles are crystalline nature [36,16]. In our case, we have observed that the spherical morphology is due to probable disorders of PVP linkers inside the IRMOF-3 structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and particle size analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, shape, and size of the IRMOF-3 are determined by the FESEM analysis. It is found that the morphology of IRMOF-3 is semi spherical in nature with a diameter about 160 ± 20 nm, shown in Fig. 6(a). The elemental compositions of the IRMOF-3 are determined by EDX analysis, 44.77% of C. 8.83% of N, 26.68% of O, and 19.72% Zn are present in the synthesized IRMOF-3 as shown in Fig. 6(b). The actual particle size of the IRMOF-3 is determined by the HRTEM analysis, and from Fig. 6(c) it is observed that the particles are 180 ± 20 nm in size. As a supplementary of HRTEM, DLS was carried out to confirm the average hydrodynamic diameter of IRMOF-3 in aqueous medium (pH 7.4). Result reveals that the hydrodynamic diameter of IRMOF-3 is about 250 ± 30 nm as shown in Fig. 6(d). The particle size found in DLS technique is larger than that in HRTEM and FESEM analysis. This is because FESEM and HRTEM are carried out in dried state whereas DLS measurement was performed in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for sensing of glucose and Fe3+ ions by the IRMOF-3
ce:section.ce:para: Amine functionalized IRMOF-3 possesses good optical properties and may bind materials by different possible mechanisms such as electron transfer [37] which can be explained by UV–visible spectra [38], fluorescence lifetime decay [39], and Zeta potential [40].

The UV–Visible spectrum of IRMOF-3 shows only one absorption peak at 355 nm due to n-π* electronic transition of COOH groups present in the IRMOF-3 as shown in the above Fig. 1(c). After addition of different analytes (glucose, fructose, sucrose, and dopamine), the absorption spectra shifted to the lower wavelength (330 nm) only in case of glucose addition. This downward observation was due to binding of electron donating group (two cis-diols) of glucose with NH2 as well as COOH groups of the IRMOF-3 moieties [22].

The time related single photon counting experiment of IRMOF-3 was used for fluorescent lifetime decay analysis. The average fluorescence lifetime decay of IRMOF-3 was 6.7 ns. After addition of glucose the average lifetime was decreased to 2.4 ns indicates that the dynamic quenching has occurred [22, 39]. But in case of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence quenching was occurred due to ligand to metal charge transfer (NH2 and COOH groups can donate electrons to the vacant d-orbitals of the Fe3+ ions) [41,42]. Here, no significant change in average lifetime decay of the IRMOF-3 due to strong inner filter effect was occurred between IRMOF-3 and Fe3+ ions which confirm that the static quenching was occurred [42–44] as shown in Fig. 7(a).

After that surface charge of the IRMOF-3 was analysed by zeta potential, which confirmed the binding efficiency of IRMOF-3 and glucose/Fe3+ ions as shown in Fig. 7(b). The value of zeta potential of IRMOF-3 was found to be −19.7 mV due to presence of COOH groups. After addition of glucose and Fe3+ ions, the zeta potential value of IRMOF-3 was enhanced from −19.7 mV to 1.01 and 3.5 mV respectively due to the formation of stable complex [40, 45].

In summary, the fluorescent IRMOF-3 is readily synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. Impressively, the IRMOF-3 could efficiently and effectively realize the selective glucose and iron sensing. In comparison with traditional fluorescence based glucose sensing, IRMOF-3 exhibits several advantages such as more stability for storage, cheap and more importantly it can be synthesized on industrial scale. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that IRMOF-3 directly sense glucose and Fe3+ ions without any modification. This report will extend one more use of NMOFs and open up a new avenue for design and fabrication of high performance sensing applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the DST, Government of India (SB/FT/CS-068/2013) and Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum yield calculation (S-1); Picture of IRMOF-3 in day light and corresponding UV light after addition of different biomolecules and different metal ions [S-2 (a)&(b)]; Compression between different types of MOF for glucose detection IRMOF-3, UIO-66 (NH2), MI-101 and ZIF-8; UV visible spectra of IRMOF-3 addition of glucose, and addition of Fe3+ ions (S-3); Stern Volmer equation of IRMOF-3 after addition of glucose and Fe3+ ions (S-5); Fluorescence responses of IRMOF-3 in the presence of different anions (10 μM) (S-6); UV–visible absorbance spectra of IRMOF-3 after addition of Fe3+ ions (S-4); Comparison with some other analytical method for glucose detection in human blood samples (Table S-1); Comparison with some other analytical method for Fe3+ ions detection (Table S-2); Comparison with some other analytical method for Fe3+ ions in blood serum samples (Table S-3): pH effect on IRMOF-3 (S-7). Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.07.039.
